title: A&A community survey on the future of scientific publishing - Credibility over speed, fairness over profit, human judgment over automation | Astronomy & Astrophysics (A&A)
content: Abstract: Scientific publishing is undergoing major change, driven by a shift toward open access (OA), the rise of artificial intelligence (AI), and growing demands for transparency, reproducibility, and equity. At the same time, rapid growth in article output strains editors and reviewers and means that metrics and speed can eclipse quality and rigor. To better understand how the community is responding, Astronomy & Astrophysics (A&A) commissioned the A&A Survey on Trends and Challenges in Scientific Publishing, which documents community opinion on journal choice, peer review, OA, research evaluation, and the role of AI, with the goal of informing future editorial policies and the wider conversation on sustainable, ethical, and equitable scientific communication. Distributed online in May 2025 to 28 787 A&A authors and co-authors, the 13-question survey drew 2944 responses from 69 countries by its closing date. Quantitative answers were analyzed through frequency distributions, and roughly 3000 free-text comments were grouped into recurring themes. The responses were clear. Journal quality and reputation are the most decisive factors in deciding where to publish, followed by cost.

title: A&A community survey reveals strong support for quality, fairness and human oversight in scientific publishing
content: "Nearly 3,000 astronomers from 69 countries responded to the A&A community survey on trends and challenges in scientific publishing. The survey, conducted in 2025, explored community attitudes towards journal selection, peer review, Open Access, research assessment and artificial intelligence. Its findings suggest that researchers value credibility over speed, fairness over profit, and human judgement over automation. The findings have recently been published in Astronomy & Astrophysics (A&A) in a new editorial, A&A community survey on the future of scientific publishing, authored by members of the A&A Board of Directors, the journal’s editorial leadership team, and EDP Sciences.... The survey revealed broad support for Open Access as a principle, alongside significant concerns about the widespread use of article processing charges (APCs). Many respondents reported that high APCs had prevented them from publishing in their preferred journals. Researchers expressed a preference for collective funding approaches, including governmental support and institutional agreements, rather than author-pays models. These findings are especially relevant for A&A, which publishes Open Access

title: View of From mission to market: a case study and analysis of the commercialisation of institutional publishing
content:
The recent partial acquisition of a university press by Taylor and Francis

provoked strong reaction from some quarters and walkouts by

academic editors at the press. This case, however, fits into a much

broader set of interrelated questions about the role, the marketable

'brand', and the very identity of the university press in the 21st

Century. In this article, we situate the case of this acquisition in these

wider debates around university governance, the usefulness of the

'commercial' vs 'non-profit' dichotomy, the flow of revenue from

libraries to publishers, and the lack of awareness by many researchers

around their rights as authors. We also elucidate the case for good,

participatory governance and more transparency around governance

as safeguards for the longevity, efficacy, and reputation of institutional

publishing.

title: Open : Annual Review by the ZBW
content: 2025 was a year of further development and transition for the ZBW. In an increasingly dynamic environment of digitalisation, artificial intelligence and Open Science, we have further refined our role as a central infrastructure institution for economics. Our mission remains unchanged: in times of digital transformation, we not only organise information, but we also create the conditions for reliable, transparent and increasingly digital science. The articles in this annual report demonstrate how we put this mission into practice. Our own research activities on and the use of artificial intelligence in libraries illustrate how closely research-led innovation and scholarly practice are intertwined at the ZBW. This also applies to our strong commitment to generating and providing high-quality metadata, as well as to our contribu�tions to the further development of Open Science, particularly in the areas of Open Access and open research data. At the same time, we are actively involved in science policy, where issues of data sovereignty and the sustainable and resilient availability of infrastructures – for example, for publications or research data – are becoming increasingly

title: PRC Alliance: an update – Publish | Review | Curate
content:
Following the meeting in December 2025 to discuss the Publish Review, Curate (PRC) model of publishing, we announced our ambition to establish a PRC Alliance. Its purpose is to help accelerate the adoption of PRC  and facilitate greater coordination across the diverse PRC community by acting as a mechanism for addressing shared issues and challenges. To help launch the PRC Alliance, an interim working group (IWG) was formed in January 2026. 

This post provides an overview of our activities to date and outlines our next steps.

title: Enhancing Equitable Collaborations through Open Science - Leiden University
content: To kick off the 2026 Open Science Week, we look into how Open Science can enhance equitable collaborations through this Open Science NL funded workshop. We draw inspiration from the partnership between the University of Ghana and Leiden University. A recent Memorandum of Understanding between the two universities has offered an opportunity to investigate how Open Science can strengthen the partnership and future research collaborations. Duringthe workshop, research support staff from the University of Ghana Library and the Leiden University Libraries will reflect on this.

title: Data Availability Statements in Mega Journals: A Comparative Analysis of Global and Korea‐Affiliated Publications in Health and Medical Research - Oh - 2026 - Learned Publishing - Wiley Online Library
content: Abstract: Data Availability Statements (DAS) have become a standard mechanism for promoting transparency and reproducibility in open-access mega journals, yet questions remain about how effectively they support meaningful data sharing in practice and how these practices vary across national research contexts. This study examines data-sharing practices in the medical and health sciences through a comparative analysis of global publications and Korea-affiliated articles in three mega journals indexed in PubMed Central: PLOS ONE, Scientific Reports and BMJ Open (2020–2024). DAS from 176,145 articles were collected from PubMed Central using an automated pipeline with manual validation and classified into a seven-category typology reflecting levels of data accessibility and reuse. Results indicate that although DAS inclusion increased over time, repository-based data sharing remains limited, while ‘data available upon request’ continues to be prevalent. Clear differences are observed across journals: PLOS ONE shows greater use of repository-based and in-article sharing, whereas Scientific Reports and BMJ Open rely more heavily on ‘data available upon request’. Korea-affiliated articles

title: Equity in Open Access Means Thinking Beyond Fees | Katina Magazine
content:
"Open access (OA) was originally defined in terms of access for readers. In the last five years or so, it has increasingly been recognized that access for authors is just as important: financial barriers to publication create considerable inequities between researchers worldwide. Among the proposed solutions to the problem of equity for authors, diamond OA, which does not charge fees for authors or readers, has garnered considerable interest.

But a definition of equity focused on financial barriers for authors and readers is also too narrow. Equity should extend beyond authors and readers to reviewers and editors, who make essential contributions to knowledge production, and should include dimensions beyond the financial or economic: linguistic and epistemic diversity as well as quality assurance in publishing. To advance a more resilient and diverse scholarly publication system, these dimensions of equity should be monitored with more attention than is currently lavished on citation counts and journal metrics."

title: How Can Nonprofit Presses Advance the Diamond Ecosystem? | Katina Magazine
content:
"In January 2026, three nonprofit presses—Annual Reviews (note: Annual Reviews is the publisher of Katina), Michigan Publishing, and Cambridge University Press—convened a small, informal workshop in Berlin to explore a shared question: How might established nonprofit presses helpfully meet existing needs in diamond publishing, particularly for smaller institutional and community-based publishers?

We convened the workshop due to a shared interest in contributing to the global advancement of diamond OA publishing and a desire to better understand which publishing services might add value to the diamond ecosystem."

title: NCI’s Office of Data Sharing builds momentum to advance cancer research and care | JNCI: Journal of the National Cancer Institute | Oxford Academic
content: Abstract: The National Cancer Institute’s (NCI’s) Office of Data Sharing (ODS), established in 2018, advances a comprehensive vision and strategy to ensure that the scientific outputs of NCI-supported research maximally benefit the cancer community and are in alignment with federal data management, sharing, and public access expectations. NCI’s large portfolio of scientific research spans the full continuum of cancer science from basic and population studies to translational and clinical research and generates a diverse landscape of research outputs including data, publications, clinical trials, tools, and resources. The NCI ODS serves as a critical coordinator across this ecosystem to steward NCI’s data sharing strategy during all phases of the research data lifecycle, engaging investigators, institutions, program staff, and technology partners via 3 pillars: Policy, Program, and Process. The office leads the NCI implementation of data sharing and public access policies, supports programs that identify and promote high-value research outputs with broad potential for reuse, and designs and manages user-centered processes for cancer data access and submission to NIH

title: The open access future cannot be a more expensive version of past - Research Professional News
content: "It’s time to move beyond transformative agreements, says Cern’s Kamran Naim For more than two decades, open access has been framed as a battle over access to journal articles: who can read, who can publish and who pays. That framing was necessary but it is no longer sufficient. At Cern, Europe’s organisation for nuclear physics research, based in Geneva, Switzerland, we have learned that the real challenge is not simply making articles free to read. It is building sustainable, trusted systems through which knowledge can be created, reviewed, shared, preserved and reused as a global public good. This requires a fundamental shift in mindset: from buying publishing services to governing scholarly infrastructure. Transformative agreements helped accelerate the transition away from subscriptions. They created pragmatic pathways for institutions to redirect existing spending towards open access publishing. In many cases, they served an important purpose: reducing friction for researchers and accelerating compliance with funder mandates. But they were always a bridge—not the destination. Too often, transformative agreements have preserved the same structural problems they were meant to

title: Erasmus University Rotterdam Joins OpenAIRE: A Conversation with Maria Constantin - OpenAIRE Blog
content:
"Following the March General Assembly, Erasmus University Rotterdam (EUR) has joined the OpenAIRE network, further strengthening a community committed to advancing Open Science across Europe.

To mark the occasion, we spoke with Maria Constantin, Open Access Officer at EUR and lead of the Diamond Open Access Expertise Centre. In this interview, she shares her perspective on Diamond Open Access, emerging publication models, and the practical realities of making knowledge more accessible."

title: Key decision points for repositories requesting reviews from external services
content: "This document was developed by Martin Klein and the COAR Notify team as part of the COAR Notify Project. The decision points outlined here emerged from interviews with early adopter repositories — Digital CSIC, HAL, and RepositoriUM. We recognise that this is just a first step in helping repositories adopt the practices needed to participate in PRC and many of these points will require further community exploration and eventually the development of shared practices. In other cases, some decisions will inevitably be shaped by local needs, either those of the communities being served or the implementing repositories themselves. In the coming months, the COAR Notify team will elaborate on the more complex issues and develop fuller guidance. The information in this document is most relevant for repositories that are using COAR Notify, which most major open source platforms now support."

title: The hare and the tortoise: Open access publications' immediate impact and lasting advantage - Taylor - Journal of the Association for Information Science and Technology - Wiley Online Library
content: Abstract: Open Access has changed how research is published and discovered. Studies generally report that OA articles are cited and mentioned more often than non-OA, describing an open access advantage (OAA). The mechanisms causing the OAA are under-investigated: this research analyzes citation and altmetrics post-publication, reporting on the development of OAAs over time. A set of journal articles (N = 44.4 M) is analyzed using seven metrics (citations from journal articles and six altmetrics), using a longitudinal methodology. These data are presented as a ratio of OA:non-OA. OAAs are confirmed for many metrics: early advantages are strong for citation and policy metrics, moderate for news and blogs, and mostly absent for patent citations. Many OAAs are sustained in the years post-publication, although citation OAAs, which typically drop in the year after publication, grow strongly in the following years. OAAs do not generally disappear over time, with the exception of policy citations. Most OAAs appear shortly after publication, and although some develop over time, they are generally sustained in subsequent years. The evidence favors an explanation associated with early

title: Allied for Open Science: special projects across Católica Libraries - Ciência-UCP
content: Abstract:  This presentation explores the strategic initiatives led by the Libraries of Universidade Católica Portuguesa (UCP) to foster a robust Open Science ecosystem. Through a series of "Special Projects," the library has transitioned from maintaining isolated technical systems to managing integrated community infrastructures. Key platforms highlighted include Ciência-UCP, which manages over 19,000 Open Access outputs and 1,600 active researcher profiles; the UCP Scientific Journals portal, which hosts 26 Open Access titles including Diamond OA; and the Open Books initiative by UCP Press. By centering the library as a digital gateway, these projects enhance the visibility, accessibility, and connectivity of institutional knowledge for global audiences.

title: Born Open: Rethinking Prestige in Scholarly Book Publishing – Language Science Press – Open Access Books Network
content: "Our authors come from prestigious institutions like the Max Planck Institute, MIT or Berkeley. LangSci books have won many awards, including all three awards given out by the Association of Linguistic Typology. We have also won the award given by the International PhoneticAssociation several times, as well as the Leonard Bloomfield Book Award, which is the highest prize you can receive in linguistics. From the outset, our goal was to become a prestigious publisher, who could compete with the most established publishers in the field of linguistics. We called this the “Champions League”. We used a three-pronged approach of innovation, excellent typography, and “big names” in the beginning to signal and give credence to our claims. Prestige begets prestige, and once you are perceived as prestigious, people submit their best manuscripts to you, initiating a virtuous circle. Today we can say that we have achieved that goal and are already the go-to publisher in some subfields of linguistics, such as, for instance, typology and language description. In closing, we invite you to read this interview with two prizewinning authors, Shelece Easterday and Nadine Grimm, who reflect on why they

title: Dutch Research Council strengthens support for DOAJ – DOAJ Blog
content:
"DOAJ is delighted to announce that the Dutch Research Council (NWO) has increased its financial support for DOAJ as a core open scholarly infrastructure for the 2027-2029 period.

NWO is a long-standing supporter of DOAJ and an advocate for equitable and sustainable approaches to scholarly publishing. The increased funding will enable DOAJ to expand its support for Diamond open access journals in the Netherlands and to work more closely with the Dutch Diamond Open Access Expertise Centre, which serves as a national hub for knowledge exchange, training, infrastructure development, and community building around Diamond open access publishing."

title: Open Research Bulletin
content: "The Open Research Bulletin (ORB), launched in 2026 by the University of Sussex's Open Research Group, aims to be a dynamic resource that addresses the evolving needs of researchers worldwide. It provides timely updates and context on the latest standards, policies, and best practices in open research. With a diverse readership, ORB is dedicated to supporting the global research community and those who champion open research. Each issue focuses on a distinct theme related to Open Research, and features a diverse collection of articles that explore key topics and offer practical guidance. These resources are designed to enhance researchers' understanding and fluency in open research principles, helping them navigate and adopt best practices with confidence. In our News & Events section, we spotlight recent awards and recognitions given to individuals who are advancing the awareness and implementation of open research practices. We also invite readers from around the globe to share announcements about recent accolades or upcoming open research-related events by emailing us at open.research.bulletin@sussex.ac.uk."

title: Overview of Netherlands University Presses and their compliance with the Diamond Open Access Standard | Zenodo
content: The expertise center enouraged that Netherlands University Presses (NUPs) take the DOAS self-assessment developed by DIAMAS, and now part of the EDCH. The diamond open access standard (DOAS) is a benchmarking resource for institutional publishers and service providers (IPSPs) of diamond open access, providing 1) comprehensive guidelines to scholarly standards and 2) a self-assessment tool to evaluate publishing standards. The expertise center has made an overview of the key results of the self-assessment, including highlights of areas the NUPs perform well and areas for improvement. The main results show NUPs perform well and have scored high on most governance and policy aspects, especially in the components of Open Science and legal ownership. A number of items in two operational areas can be improved by these, mostly new presses based on results: 1) editorial management, editorial quality and research integrity and 2) visibility, communication, marketing, and impact.

title: Open Access journals published in Belgium without author fees – The Scholarly Tales
content:
A first version of the dataset dedicated to the landscape of Belgian Open Access (OA) journals which do not charge author fees has been published with DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20760254.

This overview, compiled by Laura Mesotten and Demmy Verbeke, provides a comprehensive list of Belgian Diamond OA journals – defined as journals that are published in OA without charging author fees and that are governed by the academic community. The dataset includes a range of bibliographical, editorial, and technical details, such as ISSNs, publishing software used, funding sources, and peer review status. In addition, the dataset documents the operational Diamond OA criteria for journals as developed in the context of the DIAMAS and CRAFT-OA projects. As such, it serves as a practical tool for identifying journals that are eligible for inclusion in the Diamond Discovery Hub maintained by the European Diamond Capacity Hub.

title: The Belgian Diamond Open Access Landscape | Zenodo
content:
This dataset of Belgian Diamond Open Access journals is produced and maintained by Laura Mesotten and Demmy Verbeke. It includes journals that meet the dataset’s defined criteria for both Diamond Open Access and Belgian (see the “About” section for terminology, scope, and methodology).

The dataset reflects the state of the data as reviewed at a specific point in time, indicated in the “About” section. As journal policies, platforms, and indexing statuses may change over time, the dataset should be interpreted as a snapshot rather than a definitive or exhaustive list.

While efforts were made to be as complete as possible (within the defined parameters), some journals may be missing, outdated, or require correction. Please contact the authors with any additions, corrections, or remarks.

The dataset is intended to support research and analysis on scholarly communication and Diamond Open Access publishing in Belgium. It will be periodically updated, and users are encouraged to consult the most recent version.

title: Open Access Scenario Analyses (OASA) - Open Access Scenario Analyses (OASA)
content:
"The Open Access Scenario Analyses (OASA) project addresses current challenges in the financial and organizational dimensions of the open access transformation through various analyses and provides recommendations for the continued development of this transformation in Germany. The goal of the OASA project is to analyze various financial pathways for open access development and to assess their potential impacts.

Through systematic examination based on scenarios, a comprehensive understanding of the opportunities, challenges, and risks of advancing the open access transformation is to be gained."

title: Goals and evaluations of transformative agreements: an exploratory study of consortial approaches | Insights
content: Abstract: Since the first transformative agreement (TA) in 2015, engagement in TAs has increased, as evidenced by the growing number of agreements in the Efficiencies Standards for Article Charges (ESAC) TA registry. This study aims to determine whether library consortia involved in TAs have clear goals and if they assess the agreements they negotiate. These two questions are answered by a literature review and an examination of websites and reports from 18 consortia with 15 or more TAs in the ESAC registry. The findings indicate that most consortia in the sample had a defined goal and had undertaken some form of assessment, although the depth and breadth of both varies widely. Some of the main themes that emerged were cost-neutrality, an increase in open access (OA) papers and a desire to pay for publishing rather than subscriptions. A key takeaway of this investigation was the importance of evaluating each agreement individually, due to the vast differences between TAs. An overarching theme was the role of research assessment practices, journal prestige and the perceived need for researchers to publish in certain publications – all factors that constrain libraries’ decisions to

title: How Interactive Workshops Kickstarted OER Creation During Summer Sprint | IU Libraries Blogs
content:
"Creating Open Educational Resources (OER) can feel like a daunting task. From understanding open licensing to navigating publishing platforms and designing accessible materials, the process often raises the same question: Where do I begin? This summer, the 2026 Summer Sprint for Zero Textbook Costs cohort demonstrated that one of the best ways to answer that question is by bringing people together to learn these foundational concepts.  

Rather than asking faculty to tackle OER creation independently, the Summer Sprint was designed as an interactive, collaborative experience that combined practical workshops with dedicated working time. Using a fast-paced approach, over a two-day period, participants learned the fundamentals of open education but also had opportunities to immediately apply what they learned to their own projects. Thus, kickstarting their projects!"

title: Open Access Journals in the Field of Chemistry: An Analytical Study of the DOAJ: Collection Management: Vol 0, No 0
content: Abstract: The main purpose of the study was to analyze various aspects of open-access journals in the field of chemistry listed in the Directory of Open Access Journals (DOAJ). The DOAJ is a platform that provides access to open-access journals that have undergone quality control processes. It serves as a comprehensive directory cataloging open-access scientific and scholarly journals across various subject categories. A survey methodology is well suited to investigating the evolution and current state of open-access journals in chemistry, following a comprehensive analysis. The study identified 315 open-access chemistry journals listed in the DOAJ, published in 16 languages by 101 publishers across 48 countries globally. General Chemistry (175) has the highest number of journals in the subdomain, followed by Organic & Bio-Chemistry (85). English is the predominant language of publication, with 295 journals exclusively using it. Switzerland ranks first with 51 publications in the DOAJ of Chemistry, followed by the United Kingdom with 46 journals. Most journals impose manuscript processing fees, while nearly one-third do not levy any charges. Moreover, nearly all journals use the

title: A decade of open access: Celebrating 10 years as a DOAJ Ambassador and Managing Editor for the MENA Region – DOAJ Blog
content: "It is hard to believe that a full decade has passed since I first stepped into my role with the Directory of Open Access Journals (DOAJ). Looking back at where we started, the landscape of scholarly publishing in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region looked vastly different. Today, as I mark my 10th anniversary serving as both a DOAJ Ambassador and a Managing Editor, I am filled with immense pride for how far our regional research community has come and a sharp clarity about the work that still lies ahead."

title: Infrastructure as a shared responsibility: towards an ORI commons | Zenodo
content: Every infrastructure choice is also a values choice. The metadata standards we adopt, the repositories we federate, the data pipelines we build - these are not neutral technical decisions. They encode assumptions about who produces knowledge, how that knowledge is preserved, and who governs the systems that make it findable and usable over time.

title: Towards Open Research Infrastructure | Zenodo
content: Open research infrastructure is no longer a niche concern – it's a strategic priority for libraries, funders, and institutions worldwide. But building and sustaining that infrastructure is harder than it looks. Who pays? Who governs? And how do we ensure that openness doesn't just benefit well-resourced institutions? This talk offers a grounded look at the current landscape of open infrastructure – including nonprofit knowledge sources, persistent identifiers, and emerging data hubs – with an eye toward what's working, what's fragile, and what the Netherlands' own investments can learn from global experience.

title: RINN ORI summer event - workshop on sustainability | Zenodo
content:
The workshop focuses on a practical question facing research-performing organisations: how can institutions move beyond using open infrastructures to actively sustaining them?

Topics include:


	An overview of common sustainability and funding models used by infrastructures such as OpenAlex, OpenAIRE, Crossref, ORCID, ROR and DSpace.
	Different forms of institutional support, including financial contributions, governance participation and community contributions.
	Factors that influence institutional investment decisions and barriers to long-term support.
	Concrete examples of OpenAlex and OpenAIRE membership models and the value they provide.
	Discussion of sustainability at ecosystem level, including the potential roles of SURF, UKB, UNL, SHB and Open Science NL within the Dutch landscape.
	System-level initiatives such as SCOSS, Invest in Open Infrastructure (IOI), POSI and COMET, with emphasis on governance, funding mechanisms and collective responsibility.

title: In the spirit of Ubuntu: My ten-year journey with DOAJ – DOAJ Blog
content:
"During June 2026, DOAJ celebrates ten years of the Ambassador Programme.  In this very special post, Ambassador for Southern Africa Ina Smith reflects on her DOAJ journey.

As I write tonight, I want to admit that this is deeply personal and emotional. My heart aches when I think of the history of our country, South Africa, and how people were excluded, rejected and denied access to knowledge based on so many different factors. It is for this reason that I am taking this moment to pause, to look beyond the visible DOAJ activities – the meetings, workshops, outputs, reports and presentations – and to reflect on who I was and who I have become. Did I succeed, together with so many others, in supporting open access to knowledge and working towards a more open and transparent South Africa, Africa and world?"

title: The Lens relaunched through partnership with SPIE – Lens About
content: "On 1 June 2026 Cambia announced the sale of The Lens business and assets to a newly established non-profit based in Australia, The Lens Limited doing business as The Lens. The Lens is a wholly owned subsidiary of SPIE, the international society of optics and photonics. SPIE is the world’s leading publisher, conference organizer, and community builder in the field of optics and photonics.... The Lens is known to innovation practitioners and the research community as an open platform with linked citations and tools for discovery and analytics of scholarly works, patents, and biological sequences disclosed in patents. Since its launch in 2001, The Lens championed innovation cartography to showcase technology landscapes and open literature reviews to support researchers around the world to accelerate innovation. In October 2021, The Lens launched the Lens Collective Action project to inspire support for The Lens initiative while it transitioned from being supported by leading philanthropies to being self-sustaining through support from users. Anonymous use of Lens.org is accessible to innovators everywhere. A set of sample Lens Reports focused on technologies that mitigate climate

title: Research Data Management Practices: A Comparative Study of Research Scholars from Engineering, Science, and Arts, Humanities, and Social Sciences
content: Abstract: The need for better research data management (RDM) practices and culture within academic and research institutions is rising, and their significance in countering academic misconduct and maintaining integrity is increasingly recognized across sectors. Yet it needs to be seen how doctoral students practicing their research craft manage their data. What do RDM practices look like among early-stage researchers? The authors sought to understand how researchers use, provide access to, share, store, and back up research data, as well as their awareness of data management plans (DMPs). The authors studied a sample of researchers of engineering, science, and arts, humanities, and social sciences (AHSS) departments from select Indian Institutes of Technology to examine their data management practices. The authors designed and employed a structured questionnaire for data collection. A quantitative survey approach was adopted. It was found that science and engineering departments had slightly better data management practices and placed greater emphasis on data sharing than AHSS. Concerns about privacy, confidentiality, and fear of data misuse were the main reasons researchers cited

title: Responsible Research Assessment and Open Science in Practice: Evidence from a Spanish Research Funding Call
content: "Purpose This article assumes that embracing the values of open science is central to the ongoing reform of research assessment. Two main objectives are pursued: (1) to analyze whether responsible research assessment (RRA) and open science principles are being implemented in the Severo Ochoa (SO) call for proposals for centers of excellence in Spain; (2) to study the engagement of a selection of centers holding the excellence seal with open science practices. Methodology First, a longitudinal study of the content of the SO calls from 2011 to 2025 is conducted to identify changes reflecting adoption of the RRA and open science principles. Second, the open science practices of the selected centers are analyzed across two operational dimensions: i) open scientific knowledge and infrastructure and ii) openness to non-academic actors. Findings The call has evolved over the years, adapting its evaluation criteria to the principles of RRA: drastic decrease of the role of publication-based indicators, wider range of contributions considered and use of narrative CVs. Open science practices are increasingly promoted, particularly in centers’ strategic plans. Centers with the seal of

title: Inclusive Access and Academic Libraries
content: "Mitchell Scott (University of Kentucky) and Rachel Scott (University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign) invite you to participate in a study of the impact of inclusive access programs on library-led textbook affordability and open educational resource (OER) programs. For the sake of this study, inclusive access programs are defined as bookstore models that automatically bill and deliver all or some required course materials to students on or before the first day of class. The survey asks you to answer questions about how these programs have affected academic library staffing, services, and programs."

title: Raising Quality in Diamond Open Access - PMC
content: "In recent years, there has been considerable discussion about potential ways to improve the scholarly publishing system and bring research back to the community, especially when the research is publicly funded. Plan S (1) has made an impact on the development of national and institutional policies in the European Research Area (ERA). The goal is to develop more sustainable and equitable models in scholarly publishing by promoting the Publish-Review-Curate (PRC) model, diamond open access (OA), and the use of preprints. Diamondisation is in the focus of the European Commission, based on the Action Plan for Diamond Open Access (2), supported by the CRAFT-OA (3) and DIAMAS (4) projects, which have strengthened the scholarly publishing infrastructure and provided guidelines and standards for diamond scholarly publishing. One of the key outputs of the DIAMAS project is the Diamond Open Access Standard (DOAS), a set of criteria for institutional publishers on seven core components: (i) funding, (ii) legal ownership, mission and governance, (iii) open science, (iv) editorial management, editorial quality and research integrity, (v) technical service efficiency, (vi) visibility,
snippet: "In recent years, there has been considerable discussion about potential ways to improve the scholarly publishing system and bring research back to the community, especially when the research is

title: Launch of new online publication dedicated to providing updates and insights on the practicalities of open research | UK Reproducibility Network
content:
"Announcing the publication of the first issue of the Open Research Bulletin!

Founded by the Open Research Group at the University of Sussex and published by Open Press University of Sussex (OPUS), the ORB is dedicated to supporting the global research community and those who champion open research. This dynamic resource provides researchers and practitioners worldwide with updates on the latest debates, policies, and best practices in open research.

Each issue focuses on a distinct theme related to open research, featuring a diverse collection of articles that explore key topics and offer practical guidance, written by experts from across the open research community."

title: Goethe-Universität — Welcome to Goethe University Frankfurt
content:
"Frankfurt University Library’s Open Access publication fund offers financial support to researchers publishing in high-quality, peer-reviewed Open Access journals. The renewed DFG funding secures the continuation of this support and represents an important component of Open Access financing at Goethe University, particularly at a time of reduced public funding....

An important change will take effect on July 1, 2026: the CC BY (Attribution) license will become a mandatory requirement for funding through the publication fund. Developed by the organization Creative Commons, CC BY is widely regarded as the international standard for open scientific publishing. The license allows academic work to be shared, distributed, and adapted for both commercial and non-commercial purposes, provided the original author is properly credited. This level of openness significantly expands the reach and impact of academic publications while also providing a clear legal framework for reuse."

title: FRELIP — Open Access Discovery, born in Nigeria
content:
"We Provide Free Access to Open Knowledge & Digital Learning Resources

Our mission is to make quality education accessible to learners across Africa and the world by providing a centralized platform for Open Access journals, books, datasets, and research materials. We combine modern digital tools with curated academic resources to support students, educators, and researchers at all levels.

Through this platform, users can discover, explore, and engage with trusted academic content

We are committed to empowering education with reliable information, accessible learning, and technology-driven research support."

title: Open Science Portal
content: "The UNESCO Open Science Platform is your open gateway to the knowledge produced by the UNESCO Natural Sciences Family. It provides open access to original publications, including scientific articles, desk studies, reports and policy-oriented papers, produced or supported by UNESCO. The platform also provides practical insights from these studies through highlights, summaries and dashboards."

title: Launch of UNESCO Open Science Platform | UNESCO
content:
"UNESCO invites partners, researchers and the wider public to discover a new digital platform that promotes open access to UNESCO scientific knowledge and research products and tracks global progress on open science....

UNESCO is pleased to announce the public launch of the UNESCO Open Science Platform during the UN Open Source Week 2026, from 22 to 26 June 2026 in New York. Developed in partnership with the United Nations International Computing Centre (UNICC), the platform is released as free and open-source software and is built on open technologies, including a customized implementation of CERN's InvenioRDM, an open source framework for building large-scale digital academic repositories and platforms, as a core component. With an initial focus on research produced or supported by UNESCO's Natural Sciences Family, the platform aims to promote UNESCO-related knowledge and support the implementation of the UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science."

title: Multilingual Search Pilot | LA Referencia
content: "Scholarly knowledge is published in many languages. Yet relevant work can remain invisible when discovery depends on exact words. This guided pilot compares keyword, semantic and hybrid retrieval over a LA Referencia subset."

title: WS#15 - Embrace the Open: What do we know about reusing OE content? - YouTube
content:
"What do we know about reusing OE content? Media, format, technologies and software, logos & metadata” is the fifteenth ENOEL Workshop of the "Embrace the Open" series, facilitated by David Stöllger (TIB, Germany) and Sylvia Moes (SURF/Npuls, The Netherlands). Our guest speaker was Harry Stokhof (HAN University of Applied Sciences, The Netherlands).

Learning outcomes You will have gained insights into the following questions:


	How can we improve the reuse of open educational resources?
	What does this require in terms of content creation?
	What is the role of quality (standards)?
	How can we make open educational resources easier to find for reuse?
	How can we work together on the issues we do not yet have answers to?"
snippet: "What do we know about reusing OE content? Media, format, technologies and software, logos & metadata” is the fifteenth ENOEL Workshop of the "Embrace the Open" series, facilitated by David Stöllger
